topic: .cursorrules vs CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md 2026: Which AI Config File Should You Use?

competitors.title: .cursorrules vs CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md: Which AI Coding Rules Format in 2026?
key_points: Three formats dominate AI coding rules: .cursorrules (Cursor), CLAUDE.md (Claude Code), AGENTS.md (multi-agent)
Quick answer: Cursor-only → .cursorrules, Claude Code-only → CLAUDE.md, multi-tool → AGENTS.md base plus tool-specific files
.cursorrules has the largest community ecosystem (2000+ examples) but is Cursor-only with no hierarchy or formal spec
CLAUDE.md has the deepest hierarchy (5-layer: global → workspace → project → subdirectory → user imports) and richest formatting (Markdown)
AGENTS.md is tool-agnostic with broad adoption (n8n 178K stars, awesome-go 167K stars, LangFlow 145K stars, llama.cpp 97K stars) and Linux Foundation stewardship
.cursorrules is being deprecated in favor of .cursor/rules/*.mdc with 4 activation modes
Major projects use different formats: Next.js (CLAUDE.md), n8n (AGENTS.md), awesome-go (AGENTS.md), Deno (CLAUDE.md)
Migration guides provided: .cursorrules → CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md with specific step-by-step instructions
Recommended approach: start with one format matching primary tool, add AGENTS.md when team uses multiple tools, keep files under 200-500 lines

competitors.title: AI Coding Assistant Config Files Compared: CLAUDE.md vs .cursorrules vs copilot-instructions.md
key_points: Config file fragmentation: 5 projects × 3 tools = 15 config files — a real maintenance burden for multi-tool developers
Comparison matrix shows CLAUDE.md has the most sophisticated features: 5-layer hierarchy, global config, token visibility, memory/learning
.cursorrules and .windsurfrules have the lowest barrier to entry but no hierarchy, no global config, no token counting
copilot-instructions.md fits GitHub workflows naturally but limited hierarchy (2 layers) and inconsistent instruction adherence
AGENTS.md is the newest format, designed for agentic workflows with task-specific instructions, limited adoption outside OpenAI ecosystem
Token budget guidance: aim for 100-200 lines per config file; longer files eat into AI's context window
The fragmentation is likely temporary — either a standard emerges (like .editorconfig) or tools start reading each other's files
Writing cross-tool config pattern: start with a canonical format-agnostic document, adapt for each tool, sync regularly

competitors.title: CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md: The Configuration Layer That Makes AI Coding Agents Actually Follow Your Rules
key_points: Instruction budget problem: frontier LLMs follow ~150-200 instructions; Claude Code's system prompt uses ~50 slots; every additional line competes for that budget
CLAUDE.md offers @import syntax for modularization, path-scoped rules with YAML frontmatter (globs), 5-level hierarchy, auto-memory, and hooks
AGENTS.md stewarded by Linux Foundation under Agentic AI Foundation, supported by 60+ tools (Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Aider, Gemini CLI, Zed, Warp, Devin, JetBrains Junie)
Hooks vs instructions: hooks (.claude/settings.json) are deterministic (run linter, refuse push without tests); instructions in CLAUDE.md are advisory
OpenAI Codex uses 3-tier AGENTS.md hierarchy with 32 KiB byte limit and configurable fallback filenames; openai/codex repo uses 88 separate AGENTS.md files
Many teams maintain both: AGENTS.md for universal rules, CLAUDE.md imports AGENTS.md and adds Claude-specific enhancements
Key principle: use a linter instead of instructions for code style — 'never send an LLM to do a linter's job'
What to include: bash commands, style divergences from defaults, test/build flags, workflow conventions, architectural constraints, non-obvious gotchas
What to exclude: anything inferable from code, standard conventions, detailed API docs (link instead), generic advice like 'write clean code'
HumanLayer keeps their CLAUDE.md under 60 lines; reasonable ceiling is 300 lines; use @import for larger projects

competitors.title: How to Configure Every AI Coding Assistant: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, Cursor Rules and More
key_points: Every major AI coding tool now supports project-level configuration files, all converging on Markdown in repository root
Complete format landscape: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md, .cursorrules, .cursor/rules/*.mdc, copilot-instructions.md, .github/instructions/*.instructions.md, .windsurfrules, .windsurf/rules/*.md
CLAUDE.md reads from 3 locations merged: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (global), ./CLAUDE.md (project root), subdirectory CLAUDE.md files
AGENTS.md is the closest to a universal standard, stewarded by Linux Foundation, adopted by 60,000+ open-source projects
Codex CLI has sophisticated AGENTS.md discovery: walks from root down to working directory, supports AGENTS.override.md for local overrides, configurable fallback filenames and 64 KiB size limit
Cursor evolved from single .cursorrules (deprecated) to .cursor/rules/*.mdc with 4 activation modes: Always On, Auto Attached, Model Decision, Manual
GEMINI.md features unique /memory show and /memory refresh commands for inspecting loaded context
GitHub Copilot now supports scoped instructions with glob-pattern frontmatter since July 2025, and organization-level instructions GA in April 2026

stats.fact: Frontier LLMs can reliably follow ~150-200 instructions; Claude Code's system prompt consumes ~50 of those slots
.cursorrules has 2,000+ community-contributed rule files in the awesome-cursorrules collection
CLAUDE.md has 500+ community examples; AGENTS.md has 300+ community examples
AGENTS.md adopted by 60,000+ open-source projects, stewarded by Linux Foundation under Agentic AI Foundation
AGENTS.md supported by 60+ tools: Codex CLI, Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, Aider, Gemini CLI, Zed, Warp, Devin, JetBrains Junie
OpenAI's Codex repository uses 88 separate AGENTS.md files across its directory structure
Major projects adopting CLAUDE.md: Next.js (138K GitHub stars), LangChain (128K), Excalidraw (118K), Deno (106K)
Major projects adopting AGENTS.md: n8n (178K stars), awesome-go (167K stars), LangFlow (145K stars), llama.cpp (97K stars), Bun (82K stars)
Config file fragmentation: 5 projects × 3 AI tools = 15 config files for a developer using multiple tools
HumanLayer keeps their CLAUDE.md under 60 lines; recommended ceiling is 200-300 lines for most projects
Codex CLI AGENTS.md has a 32 KiB (32,768 bytes) byte limit per file
GitHub Copilot organization-level custom instructions became generally available April 2026
Cursor deprecated .cursorrules in favor of .cursor/rules/*.mdc with 4 activation modes

angles: The instruction budget problem: why a 600-line CLAUDE.md gives you less than 600 instructions — and the ruthless prioritization discipline needed
Config file fragmentation crisis: using 3 AI tools means 15 config files — AGENTS.md as the emerging universal standard under Linux Foundation
Hooks vs instructions: deterministic enforcement (.claude/settings.json) vs advisory rules (CLAUDE.md) — when to use each
The linter principle: 'never send an LLM to do a linter's job' — why style rules belong in Prettier/ESLint, not your AI config file
Migration paths: practical step-by-step guides from .cursorrules → CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md for teams evolving their toolchain
Real-world adoption patterns: how major open-source projects (Next.js, n8n, awesome-go, llama.cpp) choose between CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md
The convergence question: will one format win (like .editorconfig) or will tools read each other's files?
